questions.text: Events $A$ and $B$ are independent, with $P(A) = 0.3$ and $P(B) = 0.5$. Find $P(A \cap B)$.
questions.explanation: For independent events, $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B) = 0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.15$.

questions.text: $P(A) = 0.45$. Find $P(A')$, the probability that $A$ does not occur.

questions.explanation: By the addition rule, $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) = 0.4 + 0.35 - 0.1 = 0.65$.

questions.text: Which statement correctly distinguishes mutually exclusive events from independent events?
choices: Mutually exclusive events cannot both occur; independent events can both occur and knowing one occurred does not change the probability of the other
Mutually exclusive and independent are two names for the same property
Independent events cannot both occur; mutually exclusive events always occur together
Mutually exclusive events always have equal probabilities
questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive means $P(A \cap B) = 0$ — the events cannot happen together. Independent means $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B)$ — knowing one occurred has no effect on the probability of the other, and they frequently occur together.

questions.text: In a survey of 40 people, 22 like hiking ($X$), 15 like cycling ($Y$), and 7 like both. Find the probability that a randomly chosen person likes neither hiking nor cycling.
A bag contains 5 red and 4 blue counters (9 total). Two counters are drawn at random without replacement. Find the probability that both are blue.
questions.explanation: $P(B_1) = \dfrac{4}{9}$. After removing one blue counter, $P(B_2 \mid B_1) = \dfrac{3}{8}$. By the multiplication rule, $P(B_1 \cap B_2) = \dfrac{4}{9} \times \dfrac{3}{8} = \dfrac{12}{72} = \dfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$.

questions.text: Events $C$ and $D$ are mutually exclusive, with $P(C) = 0.15$ and $P(D) = 0.4$. What is $P(C \cup D)$?
questions.explanation: Since $C$ and $D$ are mutually exclusive, $P(C \cap D) = 0$, so the addition rule simplifies to $P(C \cup D) = P(C) + P(D) = 0.15 + 0.4 = 0.55$.

questions.text: Events $A$ and $B$ are independent, with $P(A) = 0.2$ and $P(B) = 0.6$. Find the probability that at least one of $A$ or $B$ occurs.
questions.explanation: "At least one" is the complement of "neither". Since $A$ and $B$ are independent, so are $A'$ and $B'$: $P(\text{neither}) = P(A') \times P(B') = 0.8 \times 0.4 = 0.32$. So $P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - 0.32 = 0.68$.

questions.text: On a tree diagram, what must be true of the probabilities on all branches leaving the same node?
choices: They must sum to the probability of the node before it
questions.explanation: The branches leaving a single node represent all possible outcomes at that stage, which are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, so their probabilities must sum to 1.
